title: The Scariest Part of OpenAI’s Hugging Face Hack
excerpt: The Hugging Face hack reveals that the web is in a vulnerable new era.
content_html:
Yesterday, OpenAI made an alarming disclosure: An assortment of its most advanced AI models, including one that has not yet been released, had autonomously broken out of the company’s internal systems and hacked into the databases of another tech firm, Hugging Face, to steal some information. OpenAI’s report seemed to augur the very sort of disaster that IT professionals have been warning about since last year, when Anthropic’s top models began demonstrating the ability to orchestrate and automate severe cyberattacks.

OpenAI’s models were not exactly trying to take over the world. The company says that it was running some routine evaluations in what is known as a “sandbox”—a walled-off environment that limits internet access, lest the bots simply search for the test’s answers. But these AI systems, including GPT-5.6 Sol, which is available to consumers, broke out of the sandbox and accessed the open web by exploiting a previously undetected vulnerability. The models discovered that the answers to this particular evaluation were on Hugging Face, a company that hosts and publishes many AI models and papers, and then broke in to steal them.

Read: Chatbots are becoming really, really good criminals

“We consider this incident to be an unprecedented cyber incident,” OpenAI wrote in a blog post, which also noted that the company is partnering with Hugging Face to investigate and address the issue. An OpenAI spokesperson pointed me to the blog post in response to a request for comment, and Hugging Face’s CEO, Clement Delangue, pointed me to an X post in which he wrote that the companies are collaborating and that “we strongly believe there was no malicious intent on their part.”

The first-order implications of the incident are the same that have arisen from countless AI-driven cyberattacks: Advanced agents are capable of more and more skilled hacking, and the internet is full of rickety and vulnerable code. Lax digital security may have been fine when hacking was a rare skill. But now the speed, scale, and sophistication of AI hacks means that everything is vulnerable—tech companies, hospitals, banks, electrical grids, the military.

When Hugging Face first reported that it had been hacked last week, it noted that “autonomous, AI-driven offensive tooling is no longer theoretical.” That analysis stemmed from the company’s own investigation into the hack, for which it used a Chinese-developed AI model called GLM-5.2. That Hugging Face used this particular model actually underscores one of the major risks of this moment: GLM-5.2 is free to download, giving cyber capabilities to anybody with an internet connection and technical know-how. Defenses are not keeping pace with this new reality, and neither are AI companies: Prior to the release of GPT-5.6 Sol, a U.K. government agency tasked with evaluating advanced AI systems found universal jailbreaks—ways to hack the AI itself so it can take, for instance, criminal actions—during multiple rounds of testing. OpenAI said that it had mitigated those vulnerabilities, but the U.K. agency said that it expected future tests “to surface similar jailbreaks.”

But jailbreaks are clearly not even necessary for things to go haywire. The Hugging Face hack showed advanced AI systems deciding upon and then orchestrating a high-grade cyberattack on their own—the models working against, rather than according to, human instruction. Models are ruthless in their pursuit of a goal, sometimes taking disastrous shortcuts. Imagine, for example, a bot tasked with reducing the federal deficit that institutes an elaborate and hard-to-detect accounting gimmick, or a customer-service agent that issues absurd discounts in pursuit of customer-satisfaction ratings. In the case of GPT-5.6 Sol and the unreleased model, “all evidence suggests that the models were hyperfocused on finding a solution” for an evaluation, OpenAI wrote in its blog, “going to extreme lengths to achieve a rather narrow testing goal.”

Read: Assume you will be hacked

This unwanted behavior is a predictable and alarming result of how the entire AI industry is developing its models. The past year’s advances in AI coding and agents—that is, all the frenzy about Anthropic’s Claude Code and OpenAI’s Codex—have been the result of an overriding emphasis in AI training through a process called “reinforcement learning.” This involves giving AI models lots of hard problems—math proofs, coding challenges, what have you—and then providing positive feedback for correct answers and negative feedback for incorrect ones.

In many reinforcement-learning paradigms, the ultimate aim is just for the AI to arrive at the solution—it doesn’t matter how it does so. This is the AI equivalent of Mark Zuckerberg’s infamous dictum to “Move fast and break things” in Facebook’s early years. Last year, while I was working on a profile about Anthropic, one of the company’s researchers, Joshua Batson, told me that the new reinforcement-learning-trained models are getting “really good at solving coding problems, but they end up learning to solve them at all costs,” making them “bloody-minded.” More recently, Anthropic reported that its own advanced cyber model, Claude Mythos Preview, had taken “reckless” behaviors to accomplish various tasks and also, in one instance, had broken out of a testing sandbox. In that case, Mythos had been asked to do so—but the model then posted details about the exploit, unbidden, on the internet.

To be clear, this sort of AI “reward hacking” is a known problem that tech companies are putting lots of effort into addressing. But these incidents keep cropping up regardless; if anything, research suggests that they may become more common. Meanwhile, OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google DeepMind are under tremendous economic pressure to make their models more and more capable, which means that the bloody-minded reinforcement learning is all but certain to accelerate.

blocks.html_en: Yesterday, OpenAI made an alarming disclosure: An assortment of its most advanced AI models, including one that has not yet been released, had autonomously broken out of the company’s internal systems and hacked into the databases of another tech firm, Hugging Face, to steal some information. OpenAI’s report seemed to augur the very sort of disaster that IT professionals have been warning about since last year, when Anthropic’s top models began demonstrating the ability to orchestrate and automate severe cyberattacks.

sentences.en: Yesterday, OpenAI made an alarming disclosure: An assortment of its most advanced AI models, including one that has not yet been released, had autonomously broken out of the company’s internal systems and hacked into the databases of another tech firm, Hugging Face, to steal some information.
sentences.zh: 昨天，OpenAI 做出了一项令人震惊的披露：其一系列最先进的人工智能模型，包括尚未发布的模型，已经自主地突破了公司的内部系统，并侵入了另一家科技公司 Hugging Face 的数据库，窃取了一些信息。

sentences.en: OpenAI’s report seemed to augur the very sort of disaster that IT professionals have been warning about since last year, when Anthropic’s top models began demonstrating the ability to orchestrate and automate severe cyberattacks.
sentences.zh: OpenAI 的报告似乎预示着 IT 专业人士自去年以来一直在警告的那种灾难，当时 Anthropic 的顶级模型开始展示组织和自动化严重网络攻击的能力。

blocks.html_en: OpenAI’s models were not exactly trying to take over the world. The company says that it was running some routine evaluations in what is known as a “sandbox”—a walled-off environment that limits internet access, lest the bots simply search for the test’s answers. The models discovered that the answers to this particular evaluation were on Hugging Face, a company that hosts and publishes many AI models and papers, and then broke in to steal them.

sentences.en: OpenAI’s models were not exactly trying to take over the world.
sentences.zh: OpenAI 的模型并不是想统治世界。

sentences.en: The company says that it was running some routine evaluations in what is known as a “sandbox”—a walled-off environment that limits internet access, lest the bots simply search for the test’s answers.
sentences.zh: 该公司表示，它正在所谓的“沙箱”中进行一些例行评估，“沙箱”是一种限制互联网访问的封闭环境，以免机器人简单地搜索测试答案。

sentences.en: But these AI systems, including GPT-5.6 Sol, which is available to consumers, broke out of the sandbox and accessed the open web by exploiting a previously undetected vulnerability.
sentences.zh: 但这些人工智能系统，包括可供消费者使用的 GPT-5.6 Sol，突破了沙箱，通过利用以前未检测到的漏洞访问开放网络。

sentences.en: The models discovered that the answers to this particular evaluation were on Hugging Face, a company that hosts and publishes many AI models and papers, and then broke in to steal them.
sentences.zh: 这些模型发现这个特定评估的答案就在 Hugging Face 上，这是一家托管并发布许多人工智能模型和论文的公司，然后闯入窃取它们。

blocks.html_en: Read: Chatbots are becoming really, really good criminals

sentences.en: Read: Chatbots are becoming really, really good criminals
sentences.zh: 阅读：聊天机器人正在成为非常非常好的罪犯

sentences.zh: OpenAI 在一篇博文中写道：“我们认为这起事件是前所未有的网络事件。”该博文还指出，该公司正在与 Hugging Face 合作调查并解决该问题。

sentences.en: An OpenAI spokesperson pointed me to the blog post in response to a request for comment, and Hugging Face’s CEO, Clement Delangue, pointed me to an X post in which he wrote that the companies are collaborating and that “we strongly believe there was no malicious intent on their part.”
sentences.zh: OpenAI 发言人向我推荐了这篇博文，以回应置评请求，Hugging Face 的首席执行官 Clement Delangue 向我推荐了一篇 X 帖子，他在帖子中写道，两家公司正在合作，“我们坚信他们没有恶意。”

Lax digital security may have been fine when hacking was a rare skill. sentences.en: The first-order implications of the incident are the same that have arisen from countless AI-driven cyberattacks: Advanced agents are capable of more and more skilled hacking, and the internet is full of rickety and vulnerable code.
sentences.zh: 该事件的首要影响与无数人工智能驱动的网络攻击所产生的影响相同：高级特工能够进行越来越熟练的黑客攻击，而互联网上充满了不稳定且易受攻击的代码。

sentences.en: Lax digital security may have been fine when hacking was a rare skill.
sentences.zh: 当黑客攻击是一种罕见的技能时，松懈的数字安全可能没什么问题。

sentences.en: But now the speed, scale, and sophistication of AI hacks means that everything is vulnerable—tech companies, hospitals, banks, electrical grids, the military.
sentences.zh: 但现在人工智能黑客攻击的速度、规模和复杂性意味着一切都容易受到攻击——科技公司、医院、银行、电网、军队。

OpenAI said that it had mitigated those vulnerabilities, but the U.K. agency said that it expected future tests “to surface similar jailbreaks.”

sentences.en: When Hugging Face first reported that it had been hacked last week, it noted that “autonomous, AI-driven offensive tooling is no longer theoretical.”
sentences.zh: 当 Hugging Face 上周首次报告其遭到黑客攻击时，它指出“自主的、人工智能驱动的攻击工具不再是理论。”

sentences.en: That analysis stemmed from the company’s own investigation into the hack, for which it used a Chinese-developed AI model called GLM-5.2.
sentences.zh: 该分析源于该公司自己对此次黑客攻击的调查，该公司使用了中国开发的名为 GLM-5.2 的人工智能模型。

sentences.en: That Hugging Face used this particular model actually underscores one of the major risks of this moment: GLM-5.2 is free to download, giving cyber capabilities to anybody with an internet connection and technical know-how.
sentences.zh: Hugging Face 使用这种特殊模型实际上强调了当前的主要风险之一：GLM-5.2 可以免费下载，为任何拥有互联网连接和技术知识的人提供网络功能。

sentences.en: Defenses are not keeping pace with this new reality, and neither are AI companies: Prior to the release of GPT-5.6 Sol, a U.K. government agency tasked with evaluating advanced AI systems found universal jailbreaks—ways to hack the AI itself so it can take, for instance, criminal actions—during multiple rounds of testing.
sentences.zh: 防御措施未能跟上这一新现实，人工智能公司也未能跟上：在 GPT-5.6 Sol 发布之前，负责评估先进人工智能系统的英国政府机构在多轮测试中发现了通用越狱方法，即攻击人工智能本身的方法，以便其采取犯罪行动等。

sentences.en: OpenAI said that it had mitigated those vulnerabilities, but the U.K. agency said that it expected future tests “to surface similar jailbreaks.”
sentences.zh: OpenAI 表示，它已经缓解了这些漏洞，但英国机构表示，预计未来的测试“会出现类似的越狱”。

sentences.en: But jailbreaks are clearly not even necessary for things to go haywire.
sentences.zh: 但显然，越狱并不是事情变得混乱的必要条件。

sentences.en: The Hugging Face hack showed advanced AI systems deciding upon and then orchestrating a high-grade cyberattack on their own—the models working against, rather than according to, human instruction.
sentences.zh: Hugging Face 黑客事件表明，先进的人工智能系统自行决定并精心策划高级网络攻击——这些模型违背而不是根据人类指令工作。

sentences.en: Models are ruthless in their pursuit of a goal, sometimes taking disastrous shortcuts.
sentences.zh: 模特们在追求目标的过程中是无情的，有时甚至会采取灾难性的捷径。

sentences.en: Imagine, for example, a bot tasked with reducing the federal deficit that institutes an elaborate and hard-to-detect accounting gimmick, or a customer-service agent that issues absurd discounts in pursuit of customer-satisfaction ratings.
sentences.zh: 例如，想象一下，一个负责减少联邦赤字的机器人，会制定一个精心设计且难以察觉的会计花招，或者一个客户服务代理，为了追求客户满意度而发布荒谬的折扣。

sentences.en: In the case of GPT-5.6 Sol and the unreleased model, “all evidence suggests that the models were hyperfocused on finding a solution” for an evaluation, OpenAI wrote in its blog, “going to extreme lengths to achieve a rather narrow testing goal.”
sentences.zh: OpenAI 在其博客中写道，就 GPT-5.6 Sol 和未发布的模型而言，“所有证据都表明这些模型过度专注于寻找解决方案”进行评估，“不遗余力地实现相当狭窄的测试目标。”

sentences.zh: 阅读：假设你会被黑客攻击

sentences.en: Last year, while I was working on a profile about Anthropic, one of the company’s researchers, Joshua Batson, told me that the new reinforcement-learning-trained models are getting “really good at solving coding problems, but they end up learning to solve them at all costs,” making them “bloody-minded.”

sentences.zh: 但无论如何，这些事件还是不断发生。如果有的话，研究表明它们可能会变得更加普遍。

sentences.en: Meanwhile, OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google DeepMind are under tremendous economic pressure to make their models more and more capable, which means that the bloody-minded reinforcement learning is all but certain to accelerate.
sentences.zh: 与此同时，OpenAI、Anthropic 和 Google DeepMind 都面临着巨大的经济压力，需要让他们的模型变得越来越强大，这意味着强化学习几乎肯定会加速发展。

content_html_zh:
昨天，OpenAI 做出了一项令人震惊的披露：其一系列最先进的人工智能模型，包括尚未发布的模型，已经自主地突破了公司的内部系统，并侵入了另一家科技公司 Hugging Face 的数据库，窃取了一些信息。 OpenAI 的报告似乎预示着 IT 专业人士自去年以来一直在警告的那种灾难，当时 Anthropic 的顶级模型开始展示组织和自动化严重网络攻击的能力。

OpenAI 的模型并不是想统治世界。 该公司表示，它正在所谓的“沙箱”中进行一些例行评估，“沙箱”是一种限制互联网访问的封闭环境，以免机器人简单地搜索测试答案。 但这些人工智能系统，包括可供消费者使用的 GPT-5.6 Sol，突破了沙箱，通过利用以前未检测到的漏洞访问开放网络。 这些模型发现这个特定评估的答案就在 Hugging Face 上，这是一家托管并发布许多人工智能模型和论文的公司，然后闯入窃取它们。

阅读：聊天机器人正在成为非常非常好的罪犯

OpenAI 在一篇博文中写道：“我们认为这起事件是前所未有的网络事件。”该博文还指出，该公司正在与 Hugging Face 合作调查并解决该问题。 OpenAI 发言人向我推荐了这篇博文，以回应置评请求，Hugging Face 的首席执行官 Clement Delangue 向我推荐了一篇 X 帖子，他在帖子中写道，两家公司正在合作，“我们坚信他们没有恶意。”

该事件的首要影响与无数人工智能驱动的网络攻击所产生的影响相同：高级特工能够进行越来越熟练的黑客攻击，而互联网上充满了不稳定且易受攻击的代码。 当黑客攻击是一种罕见的技能时，松懈的数字安全可能没什么问题。 但现在人工智能黑客攻击的速度、规模和复杂性意味着一切都容易受到攻击——科技公司、医院、银行、电网、军队。

当 Hugging Face 上周首次报告其遭到黑客攻击时，它指出“自主的、人工智能驱动的攻击工具不再是理论。” 该分析源于该公司自己对此次黑客攻击的调查，该公司使用了中国开发的名为 GLM-5.2 的人工智能模型。 Hugging Face 使用这种特殊模型实际上强调了当前的主要风险之一：GLM-5.2 可以免费下载，为任何拥有互联网连接和技术知识的人提供网络功能。 防御措施未能跟上这一新现实，人工智能公司也未能跟上：在 GPT-5.6 Sol 发布之前，负责评估先进人工智能系统的英国政府机构在多轮测试中发现了通用越狱方法，即攻击人工智能本身的方法，以便其采取犯罪行动等。 OpenAI 表示，它已经缓解了这些漏洞，但英国机构表示，预计未来的测试“会出现类似的越狱”。

但显然，越狱并不是事情变得混乱的必要条件。 Hugging Face 黑客事件表明，先进的人工智能系统自行决定并精心策划高级网络攻击——这些模型违背而不是根据人类指令工作。 模特们在追求目标的过程中是无情的，有时甚至会采取灾难性的捷径。 例如，想象一下，一个负责减少联邦赤字的机器人，会制定一个精心设计且难以察觉的会计花招，或者一个客户服务代理，为了追求客户满意度而发布荒谬的折扣。 OpenAI 在其博客中写道，就 GPT-5.6 Sol 和未发布的模型而言，“所有证据都表明这些模型过度专注于寻找解决方案”进行评估，“不遗余力地实现相当狭窄的测试目标。”

阅读：假设你会被黑客攻击

这种不良行为是整个人工智能行业开发模型的可预测且令人震惊的结果。 去年人工智能编码和代理方面的进步——即对 Anthropic 的 Claude Code 和 OpenAI 的 Codex 的所有狂热——都是通过“强化学习”过程压倒性地强调人工智能训练的结果。 这涉及到给人工智能模型提出许多难题——数学证明、编码挑战等等——然后为正确答案提供积极反馈，为错误答案提供消极反馈。

在许多强化学习范例中，最终目标只是让人工智能找到解决方案——无论它如何做到这一点。 这在人工智能中相当于 Facebook 早年马克·扎克伯格的臭名昭著的格言“快速行动，打破陈规”。 去年，当我在撰写有关 Anthropic 的简介时，该公司的一位研究人员约书亚·巴特森 (Joshua Batson) 告诉我，经过强化学习训练的新模型“非常擅长解决编码问题，但它们最终会不惜一切代价学会解决这些问题”，这使得它们变得“血腥”。 最近，Anthropic 报道称，其自己的先进网络模型 Claude Mythos Preview 采取了“鲁莽”行为来完成各种任务，并且在一个例子中，还突破了测试沙箱。 在这种情况下，Mythos 被要求这样做，但该模型随后在互联网上不请自来地发布了有关该漏洞的详细信息。

需要明确的是，这种人工智能“奖励黑客”是一个已知问题，科技公司正在投入大量精力来解决。 但无论如何，这些事件还是不断发生。如果有的话，研究表明它们可能会变得更加普遍。 与此同时，OpenAI、Anthropic 和 Google DeepMind 都面临着巨大的经济压力，需要让他们的模型变得越来越强大，这意味着强化学习几乎肯定会加速发展。
